root_cause_summary: ff_vmafmotion_init() stores user-supplied frame dimensions w/h into s->width/s->height without any minimum-size validation. The convolution functions (convolution_y_10bit, convolution_y_8bit, convolution_x) implement 5-tap filters with radius=2, computing loop boundaries such as borders_right = w - 3 and borders_bottom = h - 3. When w < 3 or h < 3, these boundary values become negative integers, causing the border-handling loops to execute with negative starting indices. The edge-clamping formula (FFABS-based, with fallback w - (j_tap - w + 1)) can produce negative array indices for undersized inputs, resulting in out-of-bounds read/write on the allocated buffer. A remote attacker can trigger this by providing crafted video frames with sub-3-pixel dimensions.
fix_summary: A 2-line guard was added at the top of ff_vmafmotion_init(), before any width/height/stride assignment: if (w < 3 || h < 3) return AVERROR(EINVAL). This rejects any frame dimension smaller than 3, which is the minimum size required for the 5-tap convolution kernels (radius=2 → needs at least 2*radius+1 = 3 pixels per dimension). The same logical fix was cherry-picked across 6 release branches; the original fix commit is 82ad1b76751b.

necessary_conditions: w < 3 or h < 3 (user-supplied frame dimensions below convolution minimum)
ff_vmafmotion_init called with unvalidated w/h from config_input_ref via AVFilterLink dimensions
vulnerable_code_patterns: s->width = w; s->height = h; s->stride = FFALIGN(w * sizeof(uint16_t), 32); without prior bounds check
borders_right = w - (filt_w - radius) evaluates to negative when w < 3
borders_bottom = h - (filt_w - radius) evaluates to negative when h < 3
FFABS-based edge clamp can produce negative indices for very small dimensions: w - (j_tap - w + 1) where j_tap > w

fix_code_patterns: Early-return guard before dimension field assignment
FixEvidence.semantic_change: Inserted a precondition check at the top of ff_vmafmotion_init that rejects undersized frames before any memory allocation or dimension assignment occurs, preventing downstream convolution functions from computing invalid loop bounds.

preconditions: w >= 3 && h >= 3 must hold before s->width/s->height/s->stride are assigned

priority_only_evidence: All 6 fix commits apply identical 2-line add_only guard at same function location
CertificatePolicy.reason: The fix is a pure add_only guard (CWE-120 pattern) with no removed code. Absence of this guard in any version means the convolution functions will receive unvalidated dimensions, enabling OOB access. The guard is the sole barrier between safe and unsafe execution; no other code path restricts dimensions before they reach convolution. cert_absent_allowed=false because the vulnerable assignments precede all downstream use. cert_fixed_allowed=false because without the guard, any w<3 or h<3 input triggers the overflow.

line_risk_signals: ff_vmafmotion_init stores caller-supplied dimensions without validation
convolution loop bounds become negative when dimensions < filter radius*2+1
Edge-clamp formula produces negative index for j_tap >> w

uncertainty: The exact CVSS vector is not independently verifiable from source alone, but the DoS outcome is confirmed by commit message (Fixes: out of array access) and CVE description.
Whether the overflow is strictly read-only (CWE-125) or includes writes (CWE-787) depends on dst[] indexing in convolution functions; the fix commit message only states 'out of array access'.

root_cause_summary: In RestoreMSCWarning() at coders/pdf.c, GetPixelIndex() returns a pixel index value that is directly cast to unsigned char (4 call sites: lines 1992, 2034, 2510, 2546). When the return value exceeds the representable range of unsigned char (0-255), the implicit narrowing conversion causes integer truncation/wraparound (CWE-190), producing an incorrect pixel value that is then written to output buffer or passed to Ascii85Encode(). Both fix commits reference the same GitHub issue #1753 and form a linear ancestry (872ffe6d0131 is the direct parent of 9dd1c7e1f8f6).
fix_summary: Two commits add an intermediate widening cast to ssize_t before the unsigned char cast on all 4 GetPixelIndex() call sites in RestoreMSCWarning(). Commit 872ffe6d0131 fixes 1 call site at line 2546 (Ascii85Encode with tile_image); commit 9dd1c7e1f8f6 fixes the remaining 3 call sites at lines 1992 (*q++ with image), 2034 (Ascii85Encode with image), and 2510 (*q++ with tile_image). The pattern is identical across all hunks: (unsigned char) GetPixelIndex(...) becomes (unsigned char) ((ssize_t) GetPixelIndex(...)).

necessary_conditions: GetPixelIndex() must return a value > 255 (unsigned char max) for the truncation to be observable
A crafted PDF must trigger RestoreMSCWarning() with pixel index values outside unsigned char range
The truncated value must reach an observable sink: buffer write (*q++) or Ascii85Encode()
vulnerable_code_patterns: (unsigned char) GetPixelIndex(image,p) — direct narrowing cast from return of GetPixelIndex() to unsigned char without intermediate widening at line 1992
Ascii85Encode(image,(unsigned char) GetPixelIndex(image,p)) — same pattern at line 2034
(unsigned char) GetPixelIndex(tile_image,p) — same pattern at line 2510
Ascii85Encode(image,(unsigned char) GetPixelIndex(tile_image,p)) — same pattern at line 2546

fix_code_patterns: (unsigned char) ((ssize_t) GetPixelIndex(image,p)) — adds ssize_t intermediate cast before unsigned char cast at line 1992
Ascii85Encode(image,(unsigned char) ((ssize_t) GetPixelIndex(image,p))) — same pattern split across 2 lines at lines 2034-2035
(unsigned char) ((ssize_t) GetPixelIndex(tile_image,p)) — same pattern at line 2510
((ssize_t) GetPixelIndex(tile_image,p)) — same pattern at line 2546
added_guards: ((ssize_t) ...) intermediate widening cast inserted between GetPixelIndex() return value and (unsigned char) narrowing cast
removed_vulnerable_logic: Naked (unsigned char) GetPixelIndex(...) narrowing without intermediate ssize_t cast
FixEvidence.semantic_change: The ssize_t intermediate cast ensures the GetPixelIndex() return value is first widened to a signed type large enough to hold the full pixel index range before being explicitly narrowed to unsigned char. This eliminates undefined behavior from implicit truncation of out-of-range values.

preconditions: A crafted PDF must trigger the RestoreMSCWarning() code path
The image must have pixel index values exceeding the unsigned char range (0-255)

priority_only_evidence: source_file coders/pdf.c contains the vulnerable pattern in function RestoreMSCWarning
mixed patch type confirms security-relevant transformation
4 direct (unsigned char) narrowing casts of GetPixelIndex() return values were all fixed with identical ssize_t intermediate cast
forbidden_hard_certificates: naked token GetPixelIndex — too generic across ImageMagick codebase
coders/pdf.c alone — file-level scope is insufficient without function-level narrowing pattern
CertificatePolicy.reason: Hard certificate requires showing the vulnerable narrowing cast pattern (unsigned char) GetPixelIndex() existing in RestoreMSCWarning at the target ref. The fix is confirmed by all 4 identical ssize_t-widening transformations across 2 linearly-dependent commits. cert_absent_allowed=false because the vulnerability manifests iff the narrowing pattern exists without ssize_t guard. cert_fixed_allowed=true because any tag containing all 4 ((ssize_t) GetPixelIndex(...)) patterns in RestoreMSCWarning is fixed.

line_risk_signals: if a tag has (unsigned char) GetPixelIndex in RestoreMSCWarning without the ssize_t wrapper at adjacent lines, it is vulnerable
if a tag lacks RestoreMSCWarning entirely, the function may have been renamed or removed — needs rename/move check before concluding not affected

uncertainty: The exact return type of GetPixelIndex() (IndexPacket) could vary by ImageMagick Q-depth configuration, but the fix is always correct regardless of Q-depth since ssize_t is wide enough for any ImageMagick configuration
The negative evidence strength for absent RestoreMSCWarning is medium — the function could theoretically be renamed or inlined in future refactors

root_cause_summary: In curl_wssl_init_ctx (lib/vquic/vquic-tls.c, wolfSSL QUIC path), four error branches call failf(...) and goto out; without assigning an error code to the result variable. If ctx_setup callback succeeded earlier (setting result = CURLE_OK), the unset result stays CURLE_OK(0), so the function returns success despite TLS setup failure. The connection proceeds without ciphers/curves applied or without CA certificate verification, bypassing peer authentication.
fix_summary: Added result = CURLE_BAD_FUNCTION_ARGUMENT before goto out; on cipher-set failure (line 378) and curve-set failure (line 386); added result = CURLE_NOT_BUILT_IN before goto out; when wolfSSL lacks keylog callback (line 397); added result = CURLE_SSL_CACERT before goto out; on CA cert load failure (line 420).

functions: static CURLcode curl_wssl_init_ctx(struct quic_tls_ctx *ctx, struct Curl_cfilter *cf, struct Curl_easy *data, Curl_vquic_tls_ctx_setup *ctx_setup)

necessary_conditions: libcurl built with USE_WOLFSSL and ENABLE_QUIC
ctx_setup parameter is non-NULL and returns CURLE_OK, setting result = 0
At least one of: wolfSSL_CTX_set_cipher_list fails, wolfSSL_CTX_set1_groups_list fails, HAVE_SECRET_CALLBACK undefined and keylog enabled, or wolfSSL_CTX_load_verify_locations_ex fails
vulnerable_code_patterns: failf(data, ...); goto out;  /* no result = error_code before goto */

fix_code_patterns: result = CURLE_BAD_FUNCTION_ARGUMENT; goto out;
result = CURLE_NOT_BUILT_IN; goto out;
result = CURLE_SSL_CACERT; goto out;
FixEvidence.semantic_change: Error paths now correctly set result to a non-zero CURLcode before goto out;, so the function returns an error instead of CURLE_OK when TLS/certificate setup fails.

preconditions: ctx_setup must be non-NULL and return CURLE_OK for the bug to manifest (otherwise result is CURLE_FAILED_INIT and function returns error)
Caller must ignore or not check the returned CURLcode for the bypass to be exploitable

priority_only_evidence: The precondition ctx_setup returning CURLE_OK is priority-only; the hard certificate is the missing result= assignment in each of the four error paths in the parent commit
The exact added lines (result = CURLE_BAD_FUNCTION_ARGUMENT, CURLE_NOT_BUILT_IN, CURLE_SSL_CACERT) are hard certificates
CertificatePolicy.reason: The four added lines are direct, unambiguous fix evidence. The parent code confirms each error path had failf()+goto out without result assignment. The ctx_setup precondition follows from dataflow: the only way result gets CURLE_OK before the error paths is via ctx_setup returning OK.

line_risk_signals: Missing error assignment before goto out in wolfSSL error paths

root_cause_summary: remoteip_modify_request() re-enters during internal subrequests (ErrorDocument/internal redirect) and re-initializes temp_sa from c->client_addr (the TCP connection address), re-interpreting X-Forwarded-For headers that were already resolved on the first pass. Because c->client_addr is the trusted proxy address, the loop treats the proxy IP as the root trust anchor again and selects a potentially different IP from the XFF chain as the useragent IP, enabling spoofing.
fix_summary: Initialize temp_sa from r->useragent_addr if non-NULL, falling back to c->client_addr only when r->useragent_addr is NULL (first invocation). This preserves the already-resolved useragent address across subrequest re-invocations, preventing re-interpretation of the XFF header.

necessary_conditions: mod_remoteip is configured with a trusted proxy (RemoteIPInternalProxy or RemoteIPTrustedProxy)
Internal subrequest / error document redirect is triggered after the first mod_remoteip invocation
X-Forwarded-For (or configured header) contains multiple IPs or a different IP beyond the trusted proxy
c->client_addr is in the trusted proxy list
vulnerable_code_patterns: temp_sa = c->client_addr; (line 254, parent commit c32f06678586) — unconditionally resets the trust anchor to the connection-level address on every invocation, ignoring that r->useragent_addr may already hold a resolved value from a prior invocation

fix_code_patterns: temp_sa = r->useragent_addr ? r->useragent_addr : c->client_addr; (replaces the unconditional c->client_addr assignment)
added_guards: Ternary guard: r->useragent_addr ? r->useragent_addr : c->client_addr — prefers the already-resolved request-level address, falling back to the connection address only when unset
removed_vulnerable_logic: Unconditional assignment temp_sa = c->client_addr; (removed, was the root cause anchor)
FixEvidence.semantic_change: Changes the trust anchor initialization from the connection-level socket address (c->client_addr) to the request-level useragent address (r->useragent_addr) when the latter has already been set by a prior invocation. This prevents re-entry from re-interpreting the XFF header chain during internal subrequests.

preconditions: r->useragent_addr must be set on first invocation (line 415: r->useragent_addr = req->useragent_addr) for the guard to work on subsequent calls
The function must be re-invoked on the same request_rec chain (subrequest/ErrorDocument)
configuration_guards: RemoteIPInternalProxy / RemoteIPTrustedProxy must be configured (non-NULL config->proxymatch_ip))
config->header_name must be set (X-Forwarded-For or custom header directive)

CertificatePolicy.reason: Root cause is confirmed by reading parent commit c32f06678586 at modules/metadata/mod_remoteip.c:254 (vulnerable line) and fix commit d0c4af10ab71 at line 258 (fixed line). The semantic change is a one-line conditional guard. No certificate expansion needed.

line_risk_signals: remoteip_modify_request line with temp_sa initialization = c->client_addr (root cause)
Request_rec r->useragent_addr check missing (makes guarding conditional on prior invocation)

uncertainty: The exact conditions triggering internal subrequest re-invocation vary by Apache configuration (ErrorDocument, internal redirect), but the re-entry path is confirmed by the commit message and the fix logic.

root_cause_summary: Unix garbage collector (unix_gc) unconditionally purges all skbs in the hitlist via __skb_queue_purge, including skbs carrying io_uring registered files. The skb destructor (unix_destruct_scm) frees the associated file references, but io_uring still holds references to those registered files in its internal file table. When io_uring later releases and cleans up its registered files, it accesses already-freed file structures, causing a use-after-free. The missing guard is the absence of any check to exempt io_uring-originated skbs from GC hitlist destruction.
fix_summary: Add a scm_io_uring bitfield to struct sk_buff (include/linux/skbuff.h), set it in __io_scm_file_account (io_uring/rsrc.c), and add guard logic in unix_gc (net/unix/garbage.c): before purging the hitlist, walk it and move io_uring-tagged skbs back to their socket's receive queue instead of freeing them; after purging, move gc_candidates back to gc_inflight_list. This defers cleanup of io_uring registered files to io_uring's own ->release path via io_ring_ctx_free.

necessary_conditions: io_uring registered files are passed via SCM (Unix domain socket fd-passing), creating skbs with skb->destructor = unix_destruct_scm
unix_gc() identifies cyclic SCM references and moves skbs into the hitlist
unix_gc() unconditionally calls __skb_queue_purge(&hitlist), which frees all hitlist skbs and triggers unix_destruct_scm, releasing the file references embedded in the skb
io_uring still holds internal references to the same registered files via io_ring_ctx->file_table, and accessing them after unix_gc freed the underlying skb/file structures causes UAF
vulnerable_code_patterns: unix_gc: __skb_queue_purge(&hitlist) without filtering io_uring skbs
unix_gc: no guard to exempt scm_io_uring skbs from hitlist destruction

fix_code_patterns: added guard sequence: if (skb->scm_io_uring) { __skb_unlink(skb, &hitlist); skb_queue_tail(&skb->sk->sk_receive_queue, skb); } in unix_gc before __skb_queue_purge
added re-inflation guard: list_for_each_entry_safe(u, next, &gc_candidates, link) list_move_tail(&u->link, &gc_inflight_list) after purge
added marker: skb->scm_io_uring = 1 in __io_scm_file_account
added bitfield: __u8 scm_io_uring:1 in struct sk_buff
added_guards: if (skb->scm_io_uring) guard in unix_gc hitlist traversal
io_uring candidate re-inflation in unix_gc after purge
FixEvidence.semantic_change: Instead of letting unix_gc destroy all hitlist skbs (which frees io_uring's registered files prematurely), io_uring-tagged skbs are returned to their socket's receive queue and gc_candidates are returned to gc_inflight_list. This defers io_uring file cleanup to io_uring's own release path, matching the lifetime of io_uring's internal file references.

preconditions: CONFIG_IO_URING must be enabled for the vulnerability to be reachable
io_uring must register files via IORING_REGISTER_FILES (triggering __io_scm_file_account)
Unix socket SCM cycle must be detected by unix_gc
version_or_feature_guards: introduced by commit 6b06314c47e1 ('io_uring: add file set registration')

priority_only_evidence: region_0004 (net/unix/garbage.c unix_gc) root_cause_score=6: added guard if (skb->scm_io_uring) in hitlist walk is the primary fix that prevents the UAF

region_0003 (io_uring/rsrc.c __io_scm_file_account): skb->scm_io_uring = 1 is the marker that enables the guard; the nearby_dangerous_operations (skb->sk = sk; skb->destructor = unix_destruct_scm; refcount_add) are the operations that create the vulnerable state
forbidden_hard_certificates: any single file path without function-level confirmation
any generic token like 'skb', 'unix', 'uring' without guard context
CertificatePolicy.reason: The fix is purely add_only with a guard sequence. The root_ cause_ score 6 region (region_0004) in unix_gc is the dominant priority signal: it directly guards against the UAF by preventing io_uring skbs from being purged. Region_0003 provides essential context showing where the dangerous skb setup occurs. Commit message confirms Fixes: 6b06314c47e1. No hard certificate can be claimed from file names alone; the guard relation must be verified at function level.

uncertainty: no uncertainty: fix commit, root cause mechanism, guard logic, and CWE are all confirmed by patch evidence and commit message

root_cause_summary: In opj_j2k_update_image_dimensions() (src/lib/openjp2/j2k.c:9221), image coordinates (x0, y0, x1, y1) of type OPJ_UINT32 are cast to OPJ_INT32 via (OPJ_INT32)p_image->x0 without any bounds check. When a crafted image supplies coordinate values > INT_MAX, the cast produces a negative value, which propagates through opj_int_ceildiv() into component dimension calculations (l_img_comp->w, l_img_comp->h). These corrupted dimensions cause downstream heap-buffer-overflow writes in opj_t1_clbl_decode_processor() (t1.c) because the decoder allocates undersized buffers based on the truncated/wrapped values.
fix_summary: Commit 024b8407392c (merged via PR #1229 in 46c1eff9e98b) adds an early-return guard at the top of the for-loop in opj_j2k_update_image_dimensions() that checks whether any of the four image coordinates exceeds (OPJ_UINT32)INT_MAX. If so, an error event is emitted and the function returns OPJ_FALSE, preventing the unsafe cast and downstream buffer corruption.

necessary_conditions: Image coordinate values (x0, y0, x1, or y1) exceed INT_MAX
Function opj_j2k_update_image_dimensions is called with p_image containing coordinates > INT_MAX
The cast (OPJ_INT32)p_image->x0 (or y0/x1/y1) produces a negative or implementation-defined value
vulnerable_code_patterns: (OPJ_INT32)p_image->x0 used directly in opj_int_ceildiv without INT_MAX validation
(OPJ_INT32)p_image->y0 used directly in opj_int_ceildiv without INT_MAX validation
(OPJ_INT32)p_image->x1 used directly in opj_int_ceildiv without INT_MAX validation
(OPJ_INT32)p_image->y1 used directly in opj_int_ceildiv without INT_MAX validation

fix_code_patterns: Guard checking all four image coordinates against (OPJ_UINT32)INT_MAX before cast
Early return OPJ_FALSE with error event when any coordinate exceeds INT_MAX
added_guards: if (p_image->x0 > (OPJ_UINT32)INT_MAX || p_image->y0 > (OPJ_UINT32)INT_MAX || p_image->x1 > (OPJ_UINT32)INT_MAX || p_image->y1 > (OPJ_UINT32)INT_MAX) { opj_event_msg(...); return OPJ_FALSE; }
FixEvidence.semantic_change: Added input validation to reject images whose coordinates exceed INT_MAX, preventing the unsafe OPJ_UINT32-to-OPJ_INT32 cast from producing negative values that corrupt component dimension calculations

preconditions: All four image coordinates (x0, y0, x1, y1) must be <= (OPJ_UINT32)INT_MAX for the cast to OPJ_INT32 to be safe

priority_only_evidence: The INT_MAX guard at line 9224-9231 in opj_j2k_update_image_dimensions is the sole fix; its absence is the vulnerability
CertificatePolicy.reason: The fix is a single add-only guard sequence at exactly one location. Its absence directly enables the heap buffer overflow. No removed code, no configuration option, no version gate. Hard certificate: the guard must be present at the exact location inside the for-loop in opj_j2k_update_image_dimensions before the (OPJ_INT32) casts.

fix_tokens: Image coordinates above INT_MAX are not supported
line_risk_signals: OPJ_UINT32-to-OPJ_INT32 cast without INT_MAX guard in opj_j2k_update_image_dimensions

root_cause_summary: ossl_ffc_validate_public_key_partial() unconditionally dereferences params->p via BN_copy(tmp, params->p) without checking whether params or pub_key are NULL; ossl_ffc_validate_private_key() unconditionally calls BN_cmp(priv, BN_value_one()) and BN_cmp(priv, upper) without checking whether priv or upper are NULL. An attacker can trigger a NULL pointer dereference by calling EVP_PKEY_public_check() with a malformed DSA public key whose parameters are NULL, causing an application crash (DoS). The root mechanism is unchecked null pointer passed to a function that immediately dereferences it via BN_copy or BN_cmp before any guard.
fix_summary: Added NULL guard checks at the entry of both functions — ossl_ffc_validate_public_key_partial() now checks params == NULL || pub_key == NULL || params->p == NULL and ossl_ffc_validate_private_key() now checks priv == NULL || upper == NULL — returning FFC_ERROR_PASSED_NULL_PARAM. Added the FFC_ERROR_PASSED_NULL_PARAM (0x40) error flag constant in include/internal/ffc.h. Added test coverage for these null-parameter paths in test/ffc_internal_test.c.

necessary_conditions: ossl_ffc_validate_public_key_partial() is called with params==NULL, pub_key==NULL, or from a caller where params->p could be NULL
ossl_ffc_validate_private_key() is called with priv==NULL or upper==NULL
No pointer-validation guard exists before params->p dereference via BN_copy
No pointer-validation guard exists before priv/upper dereference via BN_cmp
The calling function ossl_ffc_validate_public_key() propagates the unvalidated pointers without pre-checking
vulnerable_code_patterns: BN_copy(tmp, params->p) called without verifying params != NULL or params->p != NULL
BN_cmp(pub_key, tmp) called without verifying pub_key != NULL
BN_cmp(priv, BN_value_one()) called without verifying priv != NULL
BN_cmp(priv, upper) called without verifying upper != NULL

fix_code_patterns: Added early-return NULL guard: if (params == NULL || pub_key == NULL || params->p == NULL) at entry of ossl_ffc_validate_public_key_partial
Added early-return NULL guard: if (priv == NULL || upper == NULL) at entry of ossl_ffc_validate_private_key
Added new error constant FFC_ERROR_PASSED_NULL_PARAM (0x40) in include/internal/ffc.h

Added test cases calling ossl_ffc_validate_public_key(NULL, ...), ossl_ffc_validate_public_key(..., NULL, ...), ossl_ffc_validate_public_key(params-with-NULL-p, ...), ossl_ffc_validate_private_key(NULL, ...), ossl_ffc_validate_private_key(..., NULL, ...)
added_guards: if (params == NULL || pub_key == NULL || params->p == NULL) { *ret = FFC_ERROR_PASSED_NULL_PARAM; return 0; }
if (priv == NULL || upper == NULL) { *ret = FFC_ERROR_PASSED_NULL_PARAM; goto err; }
FixEvidence.semantic_change: Changed both functions from unconditionally dereferencing pointer parameters to performing early-exit NULL validation before any pointer use. The fix prevents NULL pointer dereference in BN_copy and BN_cmp calls.

preconditions: Caller must provide non-NULL params, pub_key, priv, upper pointers to these FFC validation functions
params->p must be non-NULL when calling ossl_ffc_validate_public_key_partial

priority_only_evidence: region_0003 (include/internal/ffc.h FFC_ERROR_PASSED_NULL_PARAM define) is supporting-only — it defines the error code but is not itself a root-cause location
forbidden_hard_certificates: FFC_ERROR_PASSED_NULL_PARAM token alone is not a root-cause certificate; it is an artifact of the fix
CertificatePolicy.reason: Hard certificates exist at the pre-patch source lines where BN_copy and BN_cmp unconditionally dereference potentially-NULL pointers. These are direct root-cause locations confirmed by git snapshot of parent (67813d8a4d11). The added guard sequence in the fix confirms the vulnerability mechanism. No negative evidence contradicts this.

fix_tokens: params == NULL || pub_key == NULL || params->p == NULL

line_risk_signals.signal: BN_copy(tmp, params->p) without NULL guard on params/params->p
BN_cmp(pub_key, tmp) without NULL guard on pub_key
BN_cmp(priv, BN_value_one()) without NULL guard on priv
BN_cmp(priv, upper) without NULL guard on upper

uncertainty: No uncertainty — the pre-patch code clearly shows unconditional BN_copy/BN_cmp dereference without NULL checks, and the fix directly adds the missing guards.

root_cause_summary: Two while(1) loops in QEMU USB xHCI emulation (xhci_ring_fetch, xhci_ring_chain_length) chase LINK TRBs via guest-controlled dequeue pointers without iteration bounds. A malicious guest can craft a circular chain of LINK TRBs where each link TRB has TRB_C matching ccs and its parameter points back to itself or to another link TRB, causing the loop to never reach a non-LINK TRB and spin forever — a CWE-835 infinite loop denial of service against the host QEMU process.
fix_summary: Added #define TRB_LINK_LIMIT 4 constant and per-function uint32_t link_cnt counters. In xhci_ring_fetch: if ++link_cnt > TRB_LINK_LIMIT, return 0. In xhci_ring_chain_length: if ++link_cnt > TRB_LINK_LIMIT, return -length. This caps consecutive LINK TRB traversal at 4, breaking any circular chain.

necessary_conditions: guest can write to the TRB ring in guest-physical memory (i.e., has PCI device access)
ring->dequeue points to a TR_LINK type TRB (type == TR_LINK)
the LINK TRB's TRB_C bit matches ring->ccs so the cycle check passes
the LINK TRB's parameter (dequeue target) points to the same or another LINK TRB forming a cycle
each followed LINK TRB also has TRB_C matching ccs (or ccs toggles via TRB_LK_TC but remains consistent)
vulnerable_code_patterns: while (1) { ... type = TRB_TYPE(*trb); ... if (type != TR_LINK) { ... return type; } else { ring->dequeue = xhci_mask64(trb->parameter); ... } }
while (1) { ... type = TRB_TYPE(trb); if (type == TR_LINK) { dequeue = xhci_mask64(trb.parameter); ... continue; } ... }

fix_code_patterns: added #define TRB_LINK_LIMIT 4 constant
added uint32_t link_cnt = 0; variable declaration in each function
added if (++link_cnt > TRB_LINK_LIMIT) { return 0; } guard before following LINK TRB in xhci_ring_fetch
added if (++link_cnt > TRB_LINK_LIMIT) { return -length; } guard before following LINK TRB in xhci_ring_chain_length
added_guards: if (++link_cnt > TRB_LINK_LIMIT) { return 0; } in xhci_ring_fetch before ring->dequeue assignment
if (++link_cnt > TRB_LINK_LIMIT) { return -length; } in xhci_ring_chain_length before dequeue assignment
FixEvidence.semantic_change: Introduced a hard cap (4) on consecutive LINK TRB traversal in both ring iteration paths, converting an unbounded while(1) that could loop forever into a bounded traversal that terminates after at most 4 link hops.

preconditions: the fix applies to all code paths where LINK TRBs are chased — both xhci_ring_fetch and xhci_ring_chain_length are patched

priority_only_evidence: the commit message explicitly states the vulnerability: 'Needed to avoid we run in circles forever in case the guest builds an endless loop with link trbs'
the patch is minimal, targeted, and only adds loop termination logic — no unrelated refactoring
both vulnerable while(1) loops are patched with identical guard patterns
CertificatePolicy.reason: The fix commit exists, is contained in the QEMU repo, and the evidence clearly identifies the infinite-loop mechanism matching CWE-835. The seed heuristic 'permission_capability_check' is a misfire — the actual vulnerability is a missing iteration limit on a guest-controlled circular data structure traversal, not a permission/capability bypass.

uncertainty: seed archetype misfire: vet_archetype was 'permission_capability_check' but the actual vulnerability is missing iteration bounds on guest-controlled loop (CWE-835). The heuristic mapped based on CWE description text rather than code-level mechanism.
no uncertainty about vulnerability existence or fix correctness — the evidence is strong and complete

root_cause_summary: Unbounded indirect recursion in fAbstractSyntaxNType (epan/dissectors/packet-bacapp.c) via the call chain fAbstractSyntaxNType -> fActionList -> fActionCommand -> fPropertyValue -> fAbstractSyntaxNType, triggered by a crafted BACnet packet with propertyIdentifier=2 (action) containing a nested ActionCommand with a propertyValue whose value is another abstract syntax construct, causing stack overflow and denial of service.
fix_summary: Added a recursion depth guard to fAbstractSyntaxNType using per-packet proto data (p_get_proto_data/p_add_proto_data). At function entry, increments a recursion counter read from per-packet state and returns early with an expert warning (ei_bacapp_max_recursion_depth_reached) if depth >= BACAPP_MAX_RECURSION_DEPTH (100). At function exit, decrements the counter. Initializes the counter to 0 when dissect_bacapp begins processing a new packet. Registers a corresponding expert info field in proto_register_bacapp.

necessary_conditions: fAbstractSyntaxNType is entered with propertyIdentifier==2 (action) triggering a call to fActionList
fActionList processes a context tag 0 (BACnetActionCommand) calling fActionCommand
fActionCommand processes a context tag 4 (propertyValue) calling fPropertyValue
fPropertyValue encounters an opening tag that triggers a recursive call to fAbstractSyntaxNType
No depth limit exists on the recursion, allowing unbounded stack growth from crafted nested packets
vulnerable_code_patterns: Indirect recursion cycle in a dissector without depth guard: fAbstractSyntaxNType → fActionList → fActionCommand → fPropertyValue → fAbstractSyntaxNType
Missing stack depth bound check on recursive dissection entry

fix_code_patterns: Per-packet recursion depth tracking using p_get_proto_data/p_add_proto_data
Early return guard: if (++recursion_depth >= BACAPP_MAX_RECURSION_DEPTH) { expert_warning; return offset; }
Depth counter decrement on function exit to support correct unwinding
Zero-initialization of depth at top-level dissect_bacapp entry
FixEvidence.semantic_change: Changed fAbstractSyntaxNType from unconditionally recursive to depth-limited recursive, capping stack growth at BACAPP_MAX_RECURSION_DEPTH (100) frames

preconditions: A crafted BACnet packet must contain nested abstract syntax constructs specifically: an action property (propertyIdentifier=2) whose ActionCommand contains a propertyValue that itself embeds another abstract syntax layer

priority_only_evidence: The indirect recursion cycle fAbstractSyntaxNType→fActionList→fActionCommand→fPropertyValue→fAbstractSyntaxNType was confirmed via source inspection of the parent commit; this is the specific vulnerable path the fix addresses.
forbidden_hard_certificates: No VersionAffected tags exist in the repository; Wireshark uses a release-branch model not encoded as git tags.
No CVE-specific identifier or annotation was found in the commit message (no CVE-2020-11647 reference).
CertificatePolicy.reason: The fix is an add-only recursion depth guard. The vulnerable cycle was confirmed by tracing the call graph in the parent commit. The fix does not remove code, only adds a depth cap and associated bookkeeping. CWE-674 (Uncontrolled Recursion) is the primary root cause; CWE-74 (Improper Input Validation) is secondary since the crafted packet exploits a missing invariant on nesting depth.

uncertainty: No reproducer/pcap file was examined; the exploitability assumes the identified call chain can be triggered by a valid-looking BACnet packet.
